prompt: Did the agent respond within 2 minutes of the customer’s first message (customer at 09:14:30, agent at 09:16:20)?

Is the gap between the customer’s last message (23:58:40) and the agent’s reply (00:03:15 next day) fewer than 5 minutes?
Check if the agent placed the customer on hold at least 3 separate times, and each hold lasted under 2 minutes (use the provided hold start/end timestamps).
Did the agent close the chat within 30 seconds after the customer said “thanks” at 16:44:10 (chat closed at 16:45:05)?
Verify the callback happened no later than 2 hours after it was scheduled: scheduled 13:00, callback completed 15:10.

Check whether the agent’s second response was faster than their first: first customer message 10:00:00 → agent 10:04:20, second customer message 10:06:00 → agent 10:08:10.

Confirm the escalation was initiated within 15 minutes of the customer requesting it at 17:05:30 (escalation created 17:22:00).

Did the agent send the first reply within 2 minutes of the customer’s initial message (compare the two timestamps)?
Check whether the time between the customer being placed on hold at 10:14:20 and returning at 10:16:05 was under 90 seconds.
Was the ticket updated within 1 business day of being created (use the created-at and first-update timestamps to confirm)?
Confirm the callback happened no later than 30 minutes after the customer requested it at 3:05 PM.
Did the agent exceed the 5-minute wrap-up limit (end-of-call timestamp to case-closure timestamp)?
Verify the customer waited at least 20 seconds before the agent responded after the last customer message at 09:02:40.
Was the total chat duration (from chat start to chat end) fewer than 45 minutes?
Check if the agent’s second response came more than 10 minutes after their first response (use the message timestamps).
Did the agent resolve the issue exactly 24 hours (±0 minutes) after the case was opened?
Confirm the hold time was at most 2 minutes across multiple holds by summing each hold segment duration.
Did the agent send the promised follow-up email within 4 hours of the call ending at 16:30?
Make sure the first internal note was added within 15 minutes after the chat ended (compare chat end time and note time).
Was there a gap of at least 8 hours with no agent activity between 22:10 and the next agent action (use the timestamps to compute idle time)?
Check whether the refund was processed within 72 hours of the customer’s request, accounting for the exact hours elapsed (not just calendar days).
Did the agent respond to the escalation within 30 minutes even though the timestamps cross midnight (e.g., 23:50 to 00:10)?

How many messages did the customer send in this conversation?
Count the total turns (agent + customer). Is it under 12?
Did the agent respond within the first 2 customer messages (yes/no)?
Exactly how many times did the customer ask the same question (repeat or rephrase) before getting an answer?
Check if there were at least 3 agent replies that included a question back to the customer.
How many separate exchanges happened (customer message followed by an agent reply counts as one exchange)?
Verify whether the agent used more than 1 follow-up message after their initial solution attempt.
Count the number of times the agent apologized. Was it fewer than 2?
Did the customer send multiple messages in a row without an agent response? If yes, how many consecutive customer messages was the longest streak?
How many agent messages contained a link or URL? Keep a numeric count.
Determine if the conversation hit the 10-turn limit (exactly 10 turns) or went over.
Count only the customer’s numbered lists or bullet lists. How many separate list messages did they send?
How many times did the agent ask for verification details (like order number, email, or phone)? Was it at most 1?
Tally the total messages after the issue was marked resolved. Is it more than 0?
Count how many distinct times the agent offered escalation (to supervisor/specialist). Was it exactly 2?
How many total messages are in this conversation (including the customer’s first message and the agent’s last)?
Did the agent keep the interaction to 6 turns or fewer (counting both sides’ turns)?
Count the number of customer messages before the agent’s first reply — is it more than 1?
Check if there are exactly 3 agent responses after the customer mentions a refund.
Is the number of back-and-forth exchanges (customer+agent pair) at least 4?

Did the agent ask fewer than 3 questions total across all their messages? (Count question marks and clearly phrased questions.)
How many times did the customer re-contact after the agent said the issue was resolved? (Count each new customer turn after that point.)
Confirm whether the conversation stayed under 10 total turns, where a turn = one message from either party.
Count the number of agent apologies (e.g., “sorry,” “apologize”) — is it at most 1?
Is there exactly one instance where the agent provided a numbered list (like 1., 2., 3.)?
Check if the agent referenced the policy link in more than one separate message (not multiple times in the same message).
Did the customer send at least 2 follow-up messages after receiving the tracking number?
How many distinct handoffs occurred (e.g., agent says they’re transferring/escalating)? Is it exactly 0?
Verify whether the agent’s closing/goodbye appears in the final 2 messages only (and nowhere earlier).
Did the agent keep a friendly, respectful tone throughout the conversation, even if the customer was upset?
Check whether the agent showed genuine empathy (not just scripted phrases) when the customer described their problem.
Was the greeting appropriate for the channel and situation (not overly casual, not cold), and did it set a helpful tone?
Did the agent clearly understand what the customer was asking, or did they miss the main point and respond to something else?
Is the agent’s wording professional under pressure (no sarcasm, blaming, or passive-aggressive language), especially in the tricky parts?
Did the agent provide more than one helpful option or path forward, or did they stop at a single generic suggestion?
Assess whether the agent’s explanations were clear enough that a non-expert customer could follow them without confusion.
Was the agent’s response concise while still being complete, or did they overwhelm the customer with too much detail?
Did the agent take accountability appropriately (e.g., owning what they can control) without overpromising or making unrealistic commitments?
Check if the agent used at least one moment to reassure the customer or acknowledge frustration in a way that felt natural.
Did the agent ask relevant clarifying questions, or did they ask fewer than necessary and jump to conclusions?
Evaluate whether the agent’s pacing and structure made the solution easy to follow (step-by-step vs. scattered info).
Was the closing strong: did they confirm the outcome, offer next steps, and end politely without sounding rushed?
Did the agent’s tone stay consistent, or did it become noticeably colder or more dismissive after the customer pushed back?
Overall, did the interaction feel helpful and customer-centered, or did it feel like the agent was trying to end the chat as quickly as possible?

Check if the agent showed genuine empathy (not just canned phrases) when responding to the customer’s frustration.
Did the agent clearly understand what the customer was asking, or did they make assumptions and answer the wrong problem?

Did the agent ask clarifying questions when needed, or did they push ahead without confirming key details?
Look for any passive-aggressive or sarcastic undertones—were there fewer than two lines that could be read as snippy?
Does the agent strike the right balance between being concise and being thorough, or do they over-explain/under-explain?
Overall, would a reasonable customer walk away feeling heard and taken seriously by the agent (more than just “issue addressed”)?

For chats that lasted under 3 minutes, did the agent still sound helpful and not rushed or dismissive?
